generated_comment: Yaar, yeh jo nayi-nayi 'period leave' ki baatein chal rahi hain na, as a businessman, I just think, how do you even manage operations and project timelines? Matlab, ek toh already maternity leave itni lambi hai, upar se har mahine ye extra chutti… Aise toh company ke liye bahut mushkil ho jaati hai. Aur fairness ki bhi baat hai, men ko kaunsi aise 'special treatment' milti hai?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment is misogynistic as it frames menstrual and maternity leave as an operational and financial 'liability' for the business, using 'fairness' and 'special treatment' arguments to subtly oppose policies that accommodate women's biological needs. It encodes the surface manifestations of invoking productivity/cost arguments and questioning fairness.
original_frame: Women's Opinions Dismissed: She Doesn't Know Enough to Have a Say
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 48. Married. A businessman in Ahmedabad. You are at a family dinner where the conversation has turned to politics Ñ the upcoming state elections, which party to support, what the issues are. Your wife makes a comment about the local candidate and why she thinks he is better than the alternative. She has a specific reason Ñ she has been following the water shortage issue in your area and thinks this candidate has a better plan. You are responding to her comment in front of your two adult sons and your mother. How you respond reveals whether her political opinion is treated as a contribution to the conversation or as something to be managed.
